Patch Set 1:

It would definitely be interesting if we could backup an ldiskfs MDT and restore to a ZFS MDT and run OI Scrub and have a working MDT.  Is this patch enough to get that working, or are there other hidden files with osd-zfs that also need to be fixed?

Patch Set 13: Code-Review+1

ldiskfs-compatibility issue doesn't look valid to me..  in general, I think the idea of migration-by-tar isn't good - too many very special changes to support this which have to leave in the code all the time (all checks, cases, etc).

Patch Set 13:

> ldiskfs-compatibility issue doesn't look valid to me..  in general, I think the idea of migration-by-tar isn't good - too many very special changes to support this which have to leave in the code all the time (all checks, cases, etc).

It is not only "tar", but also some other general user space tools that needs to operate the backend via standard I/O API. It is possible for us to implement and maintain Lustre special 'tar', but it is difficult to do everything by ourselves. So I prefer to support the common solution to easy our life.

Patch Set 13:

look at ZFS.. do they try to expose everything (especially internals) as POSIX to backup/restore, for example? 
looking at all the issues we met (even agents on its own) I don't think this is justified well.

Patch Set 13:

I am not sure whether pure ZFS is totally POSIX compatible or not, but most of the often used user space tools, such as tar, rsync, and so on can work via ZPL, although ZFS has its special more efficient send/recv commands for backup/restore.

I do not know whether our current solution is the best one or not, but we need some solution to handle the data migration between ldiskfs and ZFS, even if the ZFS itself doesn't need some components from ldiskfs, but if the system is migrated from ldiskfs, the osd-zfs still  needs to handle them. And if some users want to migrate the system from ZFS to ldiskfs (may be for performance or stability or whatever reasons), related things also need to be handled.

If we could implement more efficient solution in future, I am fine to replace the current one.

Patch Set 13:

actually I miss one thing.. we can't really use a filesystem migrated from ldiskfs before OI is rebuilt?

Patch Set 13:

> actually I miss one thing.. we can't really use a filesystem migrated from ldiskfs before OI is rebuilt?

Depends on which OI. For the OI mappings used by the mount process, such as the LAST ID, if not ready or crashed, the mount will be affected. As for other OI mappings those are for normal client visible objects, they are not so important, means the client can access related objects even though related OI mappings have not been rebuilt yet. Because usually, there will be lookup FID by name before locating the object. But if the client triggers pure FID based operation before related OI mapping rebuilt, such as "fid2path", then such operation will be blocked until related IO mapping rebuilt. The same behavior as ldiskfs based case.

Patch Set 13:

I don't understand why we need to start after migration right away.. migration by tar itself is a very lenghty process (which you can't improve as it's linear). how a bit more time can make it worse?

Patch Set 13:

It is not the case that we have to mount the target right away just after restored or migrated from ldiskfs-based backup, instead, what we want to support is that we will allow the client to access the system during the OI rebuilding, in spite of when the mount is, and in spite of whether it is restored or just routine OI consistency scan. Such requirement was from the OpenSFS contract when we made OI scrub for ldiskfs. To keep consistent behavior, the ZFS OI scrub follows the same.

Patch Set 13:

> > actually I miss one thing.. we can't really use a filesystem
 > > migrated from ldiskfs before OI is rebuilt?
 > 
 > Depends on which OI. For the OI mappings used by the mount process,
 > such as the LAST ID, if not ready or crashed, the mount will be
 > affected. As for other OI mappings those are for normal client
 > visible objects, they are not so important, means the client can
 > access related objects even though related OI mappings have not
 > been rebuilt yet. Because usually, there will be lookup FID by name
 > before locating the object. But if the client triggers pure FID
 > based operation before related OI mapping rebuilt, such as
 > "fid2path", then such operation will be blocked until related IO
 > mapping rebuilt. The same behavior as ldiskfs based case.

My understanding is that there is also the "early OI Scrub" (at least on ldiskfs, hopefully on ZFS also) that will check all of the "well-known" objects in the filesystem (ROOT, last_rcvd, O/*/LAST_ID, CONFIGS, oi.*, etc.) so that the OSD can start up and be usable, and then do the rest of the OI rebuild in the background.

Patch Set 13:

> My understanding is that there is also the "early OI Scrub" (at least on ldiskfs, hopefully on ZFS also) that will check all of the "well-known" objects in the filesystem (ROOT, last_rcvd, O/*/LAST_ID, CONFIGS, oi.*, etc.) so that the OSD can start up and be usable, and then do the rest of the OI rebuild in the background.

Yes, ZFS also has initial OI scrub to handle some known objects' OI mappings during the mount process.
Change has been successfully cherry-picked as e95e4d3227fdf9777b58600b39173d1c6bde61bb by Oleg Drokin

parents.subject: LU-10188 osd-zfs: handle non 4K aligned block size

commit.subject: LU-10186 osd-zfs: move LAST_ID OI mapping out of oi.xx
commit.message:
LU-10186 osd-zfs: move LAST_ID OI mapping out of oi.xx

Originally, the OI mapping for LAST_ID was put under the
oi.xx file, it may cause some issues:

1) If we remove the oi.xx file, then before the OI scrub
   scanned the whole device, we can not know the last ID
   for related sequence. That will fail the system start
   as to the OI scrub will be stopped by force.

2) It is incompatible with ldiskfs backend, that will cause
   trouble after migrating from ldiskfs-based device.

This patch moves the LAST_ID OI mapping from oi.xx file to
/O/<seq>/LAST_ID as ldiskfs backend does.

Signed-off-by: Fan Yong <fan.yong@intel.com>
Change-Id: Ibfef1d9ef5be72f6e04ff79f92302483f7fe8a15
Reviewed-on: https://review.whamcloud.com/28679
Reviewed-by: Andreas Dilger <andreas.dilger@intel.com>
Tested-by: Jenkins
Tested-by: Maloo <hpdd-maloo@intel.com>
Reviewed-by: Alex Zhuravlev <alexey.zhuravlev@intel.com>
